title: Influenza
section: Infections Due to DNA and RNA Respiratory Viruses

items.title: Understanding Influenza: Definition and Systemic Impact
items.content: Influenza is defined as a respiratory illness characterized by systemic symptoms such as fever, malaise, and myalgia. It is caused by orthomyxoviruses and is distinguished from other viral respiratory illnesses by its seasonal peaks in winter and rapid spread among close contacts.
keyPoints: Influenza causes systemic symptoms: fever, malaise, myalgia
Characterized by sharply peaked winter epidemics in temperate climates
Rapid spread among close contacts
CDC records clinical cases of influenza-like illness and excess deaths due to pneumonia and influenza

items.title: Significance of Influenza A Viruses: Mechanisms of Antigenic Variation
items.content: Influenza A viruses are significant due to their genomic plasticity, allowing antigenic drift and shift. These mechanisms enable the virus to evade immunity by altering immunogenic epitopes on hemagglutinin (antigenic drift) or reassorting genes between strains (antigenic shift), potentially leading to pandemics.
keyPoints: Influenza A viruses have eight single-strand negative-sense RNA segments
Genomic plasticity allows antigenic drift via modification of hemagglutinin epitopes
Segmented genome permits antigenic shift through gene reassortment
Antigenic shift can lead to pandemics, e.g., 1918 H1N1 pandemic with ~50 million deaths

items.title: Influenza Seasonality and Vaccine Composition
items.content: Influenza outbreaks are seasonal, occurring in cooler months with a mirror-image pattern between hemispheres. The Southern Hemisphere's influenza strains help predict the Northern Hemisphere's vaccine composition due to antigenic drift.
keyPoints: Outbreaks occur during cooler months, with opposite seasons in different hemispheres.
Southern Hemisphere strain circulation aids in predicting Northern Hemisphere vaccine components.
Vaccine composition changes yearly due to antigenic drift.

items.title: Impact and Spread of Influenza
items.content: Influenza significantly impacts public health, increasing absenteeism and healthcare visits. Transmission is facilitated by droplets and aerosols, influenced by environmental factors.
keyPoints: Outbreaks last 4–5 weeks per community but impact countries longer.
Increased school/work absenteeism, emergency visits, and hospitalizations occur.
Transmission involves droplets/aerosols; modulated by temperature/humidity.

items.title: Influenza A Pandemic Potential
items.content: Major antigenic shifts in hemagglutinin/neuraminidase can lead to pandemics. Historical pandemics occurred with new serotypes from animal reservoirs.
keyPoints: Pandemics arise from major shifts in hemagglutinin/neuraminidase.
Historical pandemics: 1918 (H1N1), 1957 (H2N2), 1968 (H3N2), 1977 (H1N1), 2009 (H1N1pdm).
Young adults were severely affected in the 1918 pandemic.

items.title: Understanding High Pathogenicity in Influenza Strains
items.content: Certain avian influenza strains, such as H5 and the 1918 strain, exhibit high pathogenicity due to factors like hemagglutinin and ribonucleoprotein contributing to enhanced replication and a cytokine storm. This immune response is particularly severe in young, immunologically vigorous individuals.
keyPoints: Hemagglutinin and ribonucleoprotein contribute to high levels of viral replication.
Cytokine storm characterized by proinflammatory cytokines explains enhanced illness.
1918 virus was of avian origin with a mortality rate exacerbated in young, healthy individuals.

items.title: Role of Swine as Mixing Vessels for Influenza Viruses
items.content: Swine play a crucial role in the reassortment of influenza viruses due to their respiratory epithelial cells having both $\alpha$-2,6-galactose and $\alpha$-2,3-galactose receptors. This allows for gene mixing from avian and human sources, facilitating the adaptation of avian strains to humans.
keyPoints: Swine have dual sialic acid receptors: $\alpha$-2,6-galactose (human) and $\alpha$-2,3-galactose (avian).
Reassortment in swine can lead to new influenza strains with human transmissibility.
2009 H1N1pdm strain was a reassorted virus from avian, human, and swine origins.

items.title: Influenza B Virus Circulation and Vaccine Composition
items.content: Influenza B viruses are more genetically stable than A viruses and consist of two lineages: B/Yamagata-like and B/Victoria-like. Since March 2020, only the B/Victoria lineage has circulated, leading to a change in vaccine composition from quadrivalent back to trivalent for the 2024–2025 season.
keyPoints: Two lineages of influenza B: B/Yamagata-like and B/Victoria-like.
B/Yamagata viruses have not circulated since March 2020.
U.S. vaccines will revert to trivalent for the 2024–2025 season, containing only a B/Victoria-like virus.

items.title: Influenza Mortality Trends and Calculation Changes Post-COVID-19
items.content: The method for calculating influenza mortality changed post-COVID-19, now using the percentage of deaths with influenza listed on death certificates. This shift reflects the pandemic's impact on excess pneumonia and influenza mortality data.
keyPoints: Mortality attributable to influenza varied between 4900 and 51,000 deaths annually from 2010 to 2023.
The calculation method changed starting with the 2023-2024 season due to COVID-19's impact.
Influenza-associated pediatric mortality is based on laboratory confirmation.

items.title: Pathogenesis of Influenza Virus Entry and Replication
items.content: Influenza virus binds to sialic acid receptors, enters epithelial cells via receptor-mediated endocytosis, and replicates in the nucleus. The process involves structural changes in hemagglutinin and is restricted to epithelial cells due to specific protease requirements.
keyPoints: Influenza virus binds to sialic acid receptors and enters through receptor-mediated endocytosis.
Hemagglutinin cleavage requires a unique respiratory protease, restricting viral growth to epithelial cells.
Viral RNA transcription and replication occur in the nucleus.

items.title: Immune Response to Influenza Virus
items.content: The immune response involves both T and B cells, with B-cell responses targeting hemagglutinin and neuraminidase. HAI antibodies measure susceptibility and vaccine-induced protection. Research is ongoing for broadly neutralizing antibodies for a universal vaccine.
keyPoints: B-cell responses target antigenic epitopes on hemagglutinin and neuraminidase.
Hemagglutination inhibition (HAI) antibody levels are used to measure susceptibility and vaccine protection.
Research is focused on broadly neutralizing antibodies for a universal influenza vaccine.

items.title: Understanding Influenza Symptomatology and Systemic Impact
items.content: Influenza presents primarily as a respiratory illness, characterized by cough, sore throat, rhinorrhea, or nasal congestion. It is distinguished from other viral illnesses by the presence of fever, chills, fatigue, myalgia, and malaise. Symptoms typically begin within 48–72 hours post-exposure, with respiratory symptoms persisting beyond the initial 2–5 days of systemic symptoms.
keyPoints: Influenza is primarily a respiratory illness causing cough, sore throat, rhinorrhea or nasal congestion.
Distinguished by fever, chills, fatigue, myalgia, and malaise.
Symptoms begin within 48–72 hours of exposure.
Respiratory symptoms persist beyond the initial 2–5 days of systemic symptoms.

items.title: Post-Infectious Effects and Physical Examination Findings in Influenza
items.content: After acute influenza, patients experience a postinfectious delay in returning to normal activity levels. Pulmonary function remains decreased for an extended period, affecting those with regular exercise routines. On physical examination, signs include sweating, coughing, nonpurulent conjunctivitis, and diffuse pharyngeal erythema. Lower respiratory involvement may show scattered rales, rhonchi, and wheezes.
keyPoints: Postinfectious delay in return to normal activity levels.
Pulmonary function is persistently decreased after acute influenza.
Physical examination findings: sweating, coughing, nonpurulent conjunctivitis, diffuse pharyngeal erythema.
Lower respiratory involvement may show scattered rales, rhonchi, and wheezes.

items.title: Influenza Pneumonia: Types and Pathophysiology
items.content: Pneumonia is the most common complication of influenza, occurring in those ≥65 years, young children, individuals with cardiopulmonary disease or immunosuppression, and pregnant women. It can be primary viral pneumonia, secondary bacterial pneumonia, or mixed infection. Primary viral pneumonia involves severe symptoms like increasing dyspnea, persistent fever, cyanosis, and significant lung inflammation. Secondary bacterial infections are facilitated by influenza-induced airway damage.
keyPoints: Pneumonia is the most common complication of influenza.
Primary viral pneumonia characterized by increasing dyspnea, persistent fever, cyanosis.
Secondary bacterial infection risk heightened in elderly and those with COPD due to de-epithelialization of airways.

items.title: Extrapulmonary Manifestations: Myositis and Neurologic Involvement
items.content: Influenza can lead to extrapulmonary complications such as myositis, characterized by severe muscle pain, elevated creatinine phosphokinase levels, and potential renal failure. Neurological involvement includes encephalopathy or encephalitis with rapid progression post-infection, transverse myelitis, parkinsonian symptoms, and Guillain-Barré syndrome.
keyPoints: Myositis is more common in influenza B, leading to severe muscle pain and potential renal failure.
Neurologic complications include encephalopathy/encephalitis, transverse myelitis, and Guillain-Barré syndrome.
Children are more prone to neurologic manifestations like febrile seizures.

items.title: Influenza and Cardiovascular Risks
items.content: There is an epidemiologic link between influenza epidemics and increased cardiovascular hospitalizations. Myo-/pericarditis, though less common, can occur as a complication of influenza.
keyPoints: Excess cardiovascular hospitalizations are linked to influenza epidemics.
Myo-/pericarditis can be a complication of influenza.

items.title: Why Rapid Influenza Diagnostic Tests (RIDTs) and NAATs are Preferred for Early Diagnosis
items.content: Rapid influenza diagnostic tests (RIDTs) and nucleic acid amplification tests (NAATs) provide quick results, which is crucial for early-season diagnosis when the circulating strain is uncertain. RIDTs detect viral antigens within 10–15 minutes, while NAATs identify viral genetic material. These rapid methods are particularly useful in managing high-risk patients or during outbreaks.
keyPoints: RIDTs provide results within 10–15 min by detecting viral antigens.
NAATs detect viral genetic material and can differentiate between SARS-CoV-2, influenza A, and B.
Rapid tests are valuable early in the season for identifying circulating strains.

items.title: When to Consider Viral Culture for Influenza Diagnosis
items.content: Viral culture is not recommended for initial or primary diagnosis of influenza due to the delay in obtaining results, which may not inform immediate clinical management. However, it can be used to confirm the strain and assess antiviral sensitivity.
keyPoints: Viral culture should not be used for initial diagnosis as results are delayed.
It is useful for confirming the influenza strain and testing antiviral sensitivity.

items.title: Why Consider Respiratory Syncytial Virus (RSV) in Differential Diagnosis During Influenza Season?
items.content: Influenza and RSV can co-circulate, especially affecting young children with bronchiolitis and the elderly with nonspecific respiratory illnesses. Recognizing these overlapping symptoms is crucial for accurate diagnosis.
keyPoints: RSV affects young children causing bronchiolitis and the elderly leading to influenza-like illness.
Overlap in symptoms between RSV and influenza necessitates consideration of both during co-circulation.

items.title: How Does COVID-19 Differ from Influenza Clinically?
items.content: COVID-19 shares many symptoms with influenza, but distinguishing features like anosmia or ageusia are less common. Testing is essential when SARS-CoV-2 and influenza co-circulate to guide treatment.
keyPoints: Symptoms of COVID-19 overlap significantly with influenza (fever, cough, fatigue).
Anosmia and ageusia may distinguish COVID-19 but are not common in all cases.
Testing is crucial for differentiating between SARS-CoV-2 and influenza during co-circulation.

items.title: Influenza Vaccine Composition Decision and Timing
items.content: The composition of the influenza vaccine is decided approximately 10 months before the seasonal peak in virus circulation, based on WHO recommendations. This timing can lead to mismatches between vaccine strains and circulating viral strains.
keyPoints: Vaccine composition decision made ~10 months prior to flu season by WHO committees
Potential mismatch due to timing of decision-making
Seasonal influenza vaccines are administered in the months preceding an outbreak

items.title: Efficacy and Effectiveness of Influenza Vaccines
items.content: Influenza vaccine efficacy varies by year, formulation, and recipient characteristics. A meta-analysis showed a pooled efficacy of 59% for inactivated vaccines among those aged 18–65 years. Vaccine effectiveness ranges from ~40 to 60% when strains match; it is lower during mismatches.
keyPoints: Pooled efficacy of inactivated vaccines: 59% (95% CI, 51–67%) for ages 18-65
Effectiveness ranges from ~40 to 60% with strain similarity
Lower effectiveness observed during strain mismatch

items.title: Advancements in Influenza Vaccine Technologies
items.content: Newer vaccine technologies, such as recombinant and cell-based vaccines, aim to improve effectiveness by avoiding egg adaptation. High-dose and adjuvanted vaccines show superior immune responses and effectiveness in older adults.
keyPoints: Recombinant vaccines approved since 2017
High-dose and adjuvanted vaccines approved for persons ≥65 years
Superior effectiveness of high-dose vaccines over standard dose

items.title: Annual Influenza Vaccination Recommendations
items.content: The CDC updates influenza vaccination recommendations annually, including age-specific guidelines and preferred vaccines for certain populations. Routine annual vaccination is advised for all individuals aged 6 months and older.
keyPoints: Routine annual influenza vaccination recommended for persons ≥6 months of age.
Higher-dose or adjuvanted vaccines are preferred for those 65 years and over.
Children <9 years with fewer than two lifetime doses require two doses before the season starts; others need one dose.

items.title: Contraindications and Precautions for Influenza Vaccination
items.content: Certain medical histories necessitate caution or contraindication when administering influenza vaccines. Severe allergic reactions to vaccine components are a contraindication, while a history of Guillain-Barré syndrome within 6 weeks post-vaccination is a precaution.
keyPoints: Severe allergic reaction (e.g., anaphylaxis) to any vaccine component is a contraindication.
Guillain-Barré syndrome within 6 weeks after previous influenza vaccination is a precaution.
Egg allergy alone does not require additional safety measures beyond standard recommendations.

items.title: Neuraminidase Inhibitors for Early Treatment
items.content: Neuraminidase inhibitors are effective when administered early, ideally within 48 hours of symptom onset. They hasten the resolution of symptoms by 1-2 days and are recommended for both prophylaxis and treatment in high-risk groups.
keyPoints: Most effective if given within 48 h of infection
Resolution of symptoms occurs 1–2 days sooner with early administration
Indicated for prophylactic use throughout the season or short-term post-exposure
Recommended for complicated influenza in hospitalized patients despite lack of formal proof of efficacy

items.title: High-Risk Groups for Neuraminidase Inhibitors
items.content: Certain populations are prioritized for neuraminidase inhibitor treatment due to higher risk of complications from influenza. These include children under 2, adults over 65, and others with specific health conditions or demographic factors.
keyPoints: Defined high-risk groups: children <2 years, adults >65 years
Other at-risk individuals: chronic conditions, immunosuppressed, pregnant women
Considered for treatment despite vaccination history

items.title: Baloxavir Marboxil Use and Limitations
items.content: Approved for prophylaxis or treatment of uncomplicated influenza in persons 12 years and older within 2 days of symptom onset. Baloxavir is a single-dose formulation but has concerns about resistance development.
keyPoints: FDA approved for persons 12 years and older
Effective if given within 48 h of symptoms
Decreases symptom duration, viral shedding, and antibiotic use
2–10% of trial participants showed reduced drug susceptibility

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a distinguishing feature of influenza compared to other viral respiratory illnesses?
options: Systemic symptoms such as fever, malaise, and myalgia.
Sharp winter epidemics in temperate climates.
High mortality rate due to bacterial infections.
items.explanation: Influenza is distinguished by systemic symptoms, sharp winter epidemics, and rapid spread among close contacts, not high mortality from bacterial infections.

items.question: What role does neuraminidase play in the influenza virus?
options: It binds to sialic acid receptors on respiratory tract cells.
It facilitates viral release from infected cells.
It encodes internal genes like polymerase and nucleoprotein.
It causes gene reassortment between different strains.
items.explanation: Neuraminidase cleaves the virus from the cell membrane to facilitate its release, preventing self-aggregation of viruses.

items.question: Which influenza virus type is most significant for causing pandemics?
items.explanation: Influenza A viruses are of paramount importance due to their genome plasticity, segmentation allowing reassortment, and extensive reservoirs in mammals and birds.

items.question: Which factor is NOT mentioned as influencing the severity of an influenza epidemic?
options: The transmissibility and virulence of the viral strain
The adaptation of the virus to its human host
items.explanation: Availability of antiviral medications is not mentioned as a factor influencing epidemic severity.

items.question: What typically triggers the need to change influenza vaccine composition yearly?
options: The availability of new antiviral drugs
items.explanation: The degree of antigenic drift necessitates yearly changes in vaccine composition.

items.question: What was a notable impact on influenza virus circulation during the first quarter of 2020?
options: Increased transmission due to warmer temperatures
A sharp decline following COVID-19 mitigation measures
The introduction of a new vaccine strain
An increase in cases among older adults
items.explanation: Influenza virus circulation declined sharply after COVID-19 mitigation measures.

items.question: Which age group is often responsible for introducing influenza within families?
items.explanation: Influenza is often introduced by preschool or school-aged children.

items.question: What is believed to be the best explanation for the enhanced illness in young, immunologically vigorous individuals during the 1918 influenza pandemic?
items.explanation: The 'cytokine storm' is considered the best explanation for the enhanced illness in young, immunologically vigorous individuals during the 1918 pandemic.

items.question: Which type of influenza virus has both $\alpha$-2,6-galactose and $\alpha$-2,3-galactose receptors on their respiratory epithelial cells?
items.explanation: Swine have both types of receptors on their respiratory epithelial cells, facilitating reassortment and host adaptation.

items.question: Which influenza B lineage has not circulated in the population since March 2020?
options: Both lineages have been circulating equally
items.explanation: Since March 2020, B/Yamagata influenza viruses have not circulated in the population.

items.question: What is a primary reason for the high pathogenicity of certain avian influenza strains like H5N1?
options: Optimal gene constellation including all eight segmented genes
Presence in military camps during World War I
items.explanation: Pathogenicity is a function of the viruses’ surface proteins and an optimal gene constellation.

items.question: What method is used to calculate mortality due to influenza starting with the 2023-2024 season?
options: Percentage of deaths with influenza listed on the death certificate.
Excess over the anticipated sine-wave curve of pneumonia and influenza deaths during the year.
Number of hospitalizations due to influenza.
items.explanation: Mortality calculation now uses the percentage of deaths with influenza listed on the death certificate.

items.question: What is a limitation in estimating pediatric mortality due to laboratory-confirmed influenza?
options: All children are tested for influenza.
Tests are more sensitive later in the illness.
Not all children are tested, and tests are less sensitive later in the illness.
items.explanation: Estimates are limited because not all children are tested for influenza, and tests are less sensitive later in the illness.

items.question: How does influenza virus enter epithelial cells?
options: Through direct fusion with the cell membrane.
By binding to sialic acid receptors and receptor-mediated endocytosis.
Via passive diffusion through the cell wall.
items.explanation: Influenza virus enters epithelial cells by binding to sialic acid receptors and receptor-mediated endocytosis.

items.question: What is the role of hemagglutinin cleavage in influenza virus infection?
options: It allows viral RNA to enter the nucleus.
It restricts viral growth to epithelial cells by requiring a unique protease.
It facilitates direct fusion with host cell membranes.
It enhances the sensitivity of diagnostic tests.
items.explanation: Hemagglutinin cleavage may restrict viral growth to epithelial cells, as it requires a specific protease in the respiratory milieu.

items.question: Which of the following symptoms is most characteristic of influenza compared to other respiratory viral illnesses?
options: Degree of accompanying fever, chills, fatigue, myalgia, and malaise
items.explanation: The degree of accompanying fever, chills, fatigue, myalgia, and malaise distinguishes influenza from most other respiratory viral illnesses.

items.question: What is the typical duration for systemic symptoms to persist in a patient with influenza?
items.explanation: Systemic symptoms of influenza typically last for 2–5 days.

items.question: In elderly patients with influenza, which symptom is often less prominent?
items.explanation: In the elderly, the respiratory presentation may be less prominent.

items.question: Which group is most commonly affected by complications of influenza?
options: Persons aged 18-45 with no underlying health conditions
Young children and persons aged ≥65 years with underlying cardiopulmonary disease or immunosuppression
Adults in their first trimester of pregnancy
items.explanation: Complications occur most commonly in young children, persons aged ≥65 years, and those with underlying health conditions.

items.question: What is the most common respiratory complication of influenza?
items.explanation: Pneumonia is the most common respiratory complication of influenza.

items.question: Which type of pneumonia involves increasing dyspnea and persistent fever?
items.explanation: Primary influenza viral pneumonia is characterized by increasing dyspnea, persistent fever, and in severe cases, cyanosis.

items.question: What extrapulmonary manifestation of influenza is more common with influenza B?
items.explanation: Myositis, characterized by severe muscle pain and elevated creatinine phosphokinase levels, is more common with influenza B.

items.question: Which type of specimen is most easily used for recovering the influenza virus?
items.explanation: Influenza virus is most easily recovered from nasal or pharyngeal specimens.

items.question: Which diagnostic test for influenza provides results within 10–15 minutes?
items.explanation: Rapid influenza diagnostic tests (RIDTs) work by detecting viral antigens and can provide results within 10–15 min.

items.question: Why should clinicians not use viral culture for the initial or primary diagnosis of influenza?
options: Results are not timely to inform clinical management
items.explanation: Clinicians should not use viral culture for initial or primary diagnosis because results will not be available in a timely manner to inform clinical management.

items.question: Which virus is particularly noted for affecting young children and causing bronchiolitis, especially when co-circulating with influenza?
items.explanation: Respiratory syncytial virus often affects young children, causing bronchiolitis.

items.question: Which symptom is more commonly associated with COVID-19 than influenza and can help distinguish between the two?
items.explanation: New loss of taste (ageusia) or smell (anosmia) may distinguish COVID-19 from influenza.

items.question: During periods when both SARS-CoV-2 and influenza viruses are circulating, what should clinicians consider in patients with acute respiratory illness symptoms?
options: Both viruses as well as co-infection
Neither, as symptoms are too similar
items.explanation: Clinicians should consider both viruses and the possibility of co-infection.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a factor considered when formulating the seasonal influenza vaccine?
items.explanation: No effort is made to standardize the neuraminidase content in influenza vaccines.

items.question: What is a unique characteristic of the timing for administering the seasonal influenza vaccine?
options: It is given annually after the peak season.
It is administered during the outbreak in temperate climates.
It is typically available starting in August or September, before the outbreak.
It coincides with the WHO's decision-making process.
items.explanation: Influenza vaccine is unique in being given seasonally in the months immediately preceding an outbreak in temperate climates.

items.question: Which of the following vaccines was approved by the FDA as the first fully recombinant influenza vaccine?
items.explanation: The first fully recombinant vaccine was approved by the FDA in 2017.

items.question: Which of the following adverse events has been associated with inactivated influenza vaccines?
items.explanation: Guillain-Barré syndrome is a rare adverse event associated with inactivated influenza vaccines.

items.question: Which age group is recommended to receive two doses of the influenza vaccine if they have not received at least two lifetime doses prior to the start of the season?
items.explanation: Children under 9 years who have not received at least two lifetime doses should receive two doses.

items.question: For which age group are higher-dose or adjuvanted influenza vaccines preferred?
items.explanation: Higher-dose or adjuvanted vaccines are preferred for those 65 years of age and over.

items.question: What is a contraindication to influenza vaccination?
options: History of severe allergic reaction to any vaccine component
items.explanation: A history of severe allergic reaction (e.g., anaphylaxis) to any vaccine component is a contraindication.

items.question: Which antiviral agents were historically used to treat influenza A before the development of widespread resistance?
items.explanation: M-2 channel blockers amantadine and rimantadine were used before widespread resistance.

items.question: What is the primary mechanism of action for neuraminidase inhibitors in treating influenza?
options: They inhibit viral entry into host cells.
They block the M-2 ion channel.
They prevent the egress of influenza virus from infected cells.
They enhance immune response to the virus.
items.explanation: Neuraminidase inhibitors limit the egress of influenza virus from an infected cell.

items.question: For which age group is oseltamivir approved for treatment beginning at 14 days of life?
items.explanation: Oseltamivir is approved for infants starting from 14 days of age.

items.question: Which neuraminidase inhibitor is administered as an inhaled dry powder?
items.explanation: Zanamivir is given as an inhaled dry powder.

items.statement: The influenza A virus has a segmented genome that allows for gene reassortment.
items.explanation: The segmented nature of the influenza A virus genome enables gene reassortment, akin to shuffling a deck of cards.

items.statement: Influenza B viruses are primarily responsible for causing pandemics.
items.explanation: Pandemics are primarily caused by influenza A viruses due to their ability to undergo antigenic shift and reassortment.

items.statement: The impact of influenza is most easily recognized in the elderly population.
items.explanation: Influenza's impact is most easily recognized in the pediatric population due to school absenteeism.

items.statement: The Southern Hemisphere's influenza strain circulation can help predict vaccine composition for the Northern Hemisphere.
items.explanation: Circulation of strains in one hemisphere has predictive value for vaccine composition in the other.

items.statement: The impact of the 1918 influenza virus cannot be attributed solely to the disruption caused by World War I.
items.explanation: The illness was documented in countries not involved in World War I, indicating other factors contributed.

items.statement: Influenza C viruses are the most genetically stable and cause severe disease.
items.explanation: Influenza B viruses are more genetically stable than A, and Influenza C causes mild disease.

items.statement: Influenza-associated pediatric mortality is based on clinical diagnosis.
items.explanation: Pediatric mortality is based on laboratory confirmation, not just clinical diagnosis.

items.statement: The onset of influenza symptoms typically follows an incubation period of 48–72 hours.
items.explanation: Influenza has a short incubation period, with symptom onset occurring within 48–72 hours.

items.statement: Gastrointestinal findings are common in patients with influenza.
items.explanation: There are rare gastrointestinal findings associated with influenza.

items.statement: Localized pulmonary findings in a patient with influenza suggest uncomplicated viral pneumonia.
items.explanation: Localized pulmonary findings suggest relatively complicated pneumonia with a bacterial component.

items.statement: Influenza can cause Reye syndrome in children and adolescents.
items.explanation: Reye syndrome was commonly seen in children with influenza, particularly those infected with influenza B virus.

items.statement: Influenza spreads beyond respiratory epithelial cells to cause systemic infections.
items.explanation: Influenza is believed to spread rarely beyond the respiratory epithelial cells, yet it causes extrapulmonary manifestations.

items.statement: Serologic confirmation of influenza infection requires only one serum sample.
items.explanation: Serologic confirmation requires paired serum samples, with the convalescent-phase sample obtained 2 weeks after infection.

items.statement: A white blood cell count >15,000/μL in influenza pneumonia suggests a secondary bacterial component.
items.explanation: A white blood cell count >15,000/μL suggests a secondary bacterial component in influenza pneumonia.

items.statement: Influenza can be reliably diagnosed based on symptoms alone during peak influenza season.
items.explanation: Less common presentations of influenza and cases outside peak season are frequently misdiagnosed.

items.statement: Testing is important to inform treatment decisions when SARS-CoV-2 and influenza viruses co-circulate.
items.explanation: The similar clinical presentations of both viruses highlight the importance of testing.

items.statement: Influenza vaccines are standardized for neuraminidase content.
items.explanation: No effort is made to standardize the neuraminidase content in influenza vaccines.

items.statement: Live-attenuated influenza vaccines are recommended for children younger than 2 years.
items.explanation: LAIVs have been associated with excess wheezing and hospitalizations in children younger than 2 years and thus are not licensed for use in this age group.

items.statement: Egg allergy alone requires additional safety measures for influenza vaccination.
items.explanation: Egg allergy alone does not necessitate additional safety measures beyond those recommended for any vaccine recipient.

items.statement: A history of Guillain-Barré syndrome within 6 weeks of a previous dose of influenza vaccine is a contraindication.
items.explanation: It is considered a precaution, not a contraindication.

items.statement: Resistance to neuraminidase inhibitors among currently circulating influenza A or B strains is widespread.
items.explanation: Resistance to neuraminidase inhibitors is not widespread among currently circulating strains.

items.statement: Baloxavir marboxil is recommended for use in pregnant women and breastfeeding mothers.
items.explanation: CDC does not recommend baloxavir for pregnant or breastfeeding women.

items.assertion: Influenza A viruses can cause pandemics.
items.reason: A major shift in hemagglutinin and/or neuraminidase introduces a new serotype from an animal or avian reservoir.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why influenza A can cause pandemics.

items.assertion: The 1918 influenza epidemic primarily affected infants and elderly individuals.
items.reason: Infants and elderly individuals are typically more susceptible to influenza.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the 1918 epidemic severely impacted young adults, not primarily infants and elderly.

items.assertion: The swine origin 2009 H1N1pdm strain was a reassorted virus with gene segment origins from avian, human, and swine hosts.
items.reason: Swine play an important role in interspecies transmission of influenza due to their respiratory epithelial cells having both types of sialic acid receptors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why swine can facilitate reassortment.

items.assertion: Influenza B viruses have two lineages that circulate simultaneously.
items.reason: Co-circulation of both B lineages began in 2011, leading to the inclusion of representatives from both lineages in quadrivalent vaccines.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason explaining why both lineages circulate simultaneously.

items.assertion: Hemagglutination inhibition (HAI) antibodies are used as a measure of susceptibility to clinical infection.
items.reason: HAI antibody levels correlate with neutralizing antibodies and vaccine-induced protection.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.

items.assertion: T-cell immunity is well understood in humans for its role in influenza infection.
items.reason: T-cell immunity plays a role in clearance of an influenza infection that develops 8–10 days after exposure.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the role of T-cell immunity remains unclear, though it does play a role in clearing infections.

items.assertion: Secondary bacterial pneumonia can occur after influenza.
items.reason: Influenza de-epithelializes the airways and destroys ciliary function, allowing bacterial contamination.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why secondary bacterial pneumonia occurs.

items.assertion: Guillain-Barré syndrome is a common complication of influenza.
items.reason: It was reported after widespread influenza vaccination in 1976.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as Guillain-Barré syndrome is not a common complication, though it has been associated with vaccination efforts.

items.assertion: Rapid molecular assays (NAATs) can differentiate between SARS-CoV-2, influenza A, and influenza B.
items.reason: Several NAATs are authorized for home use and detect viral genetic material.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.

items.assertion: High-dose vaccines elicit greater immune responses than traditional inactivated influenza vaccines.
items.reason: They are approved for persons aged ≥65 years due to their superior effectiveness.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is a correct explanation of the assertion.

items.assertion: The decision about vaccine composition must be made after the seasonal peak in influenza virus circulation.
items.reason: This timing can result in a mismatch of vaccine composition with the viral strains that are actually prevalent in the upcoming season.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the decision must be made ~10 months before the seasonal peak, not after.

items.assertion: Neuraminidase inhibitors are most effective when administered within 48 hours of infection.
items.reason: Early administration hastens the resolution of symptoms by 1–2 days.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains why early administration is beneficial.

items.assertion: Oseltamivir should not be used in patients with poor oral intake or absorption.
items.reason: Oseltamivir can only be administered orally and cannot be given by oro/nasal tube.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as oseltamivir can also be given by oro/nasal tube.
